author: @ronson-natsarim
text:
Belief is a feeling of certainty that is not based on experience / competency. Belief is illogical, never beneficial, and, I think, the #1 fault of humanity.

Faith is a feeling of certainty that is based on experience / competency and is proportionate to it. The more experienced / competent you are with X thing, the more faith you have in the consistency of that experience / performance of that competency. Faith is the logical certainty we should all strive to develop as the RESULT of developing our competency through experience.

Trust is a decision to take some action despite feeling uncertain of the result.

author: @kevgermany
text:
Perhaps part of the problem is people's beliefs ignoring facts that contradict them. 
Legionnaires disease is just one example. 
Beliefs are great. But so is judicious evaluation of factual evidence. Without getting into politics or religion, the ability of charismatic leaders to convince others to believe in falsehoods that support the leader is one of the biggest dangers facing mankind.

author: @SI00000
text:
If you can't prove your belief system then why waste precious time believing in it? 
Having said that, not all beliefs are irrational since there must be a system for us to follow that guides us to our perfection just as all animals have a unique system by which they adhere to and never deviate from.
Humans are the only species to deviate which is why there is so much chaos among us.
The answer for which belief system is true can be seen through a scientific lens as to which one not only answers your questions but which one brings benefit to humanity.
Looking into why so many social, spiritual and mental ills plague the Secular West more so than the the rest of the world will point you into the right direction.

author: @CelticShae
text:
This seems like a strange sideroad for this channel. It never fails to surprise me, yet always manages to disappoint me, just how concerned some people are that others believe the same thing as them. You work to put good in the world. That's more than marvelous and wonderful enough for me to greet you warmly and wish you well.

I always appreciate your videos and watch with great interest.

author: @sojournerdelaterra6144
text:
WOW. Talk about timing! For years I tried convincing others of the validity of some of my belief systems.  For the most part, I might as well have been trying to explain things to a brick most of the time. Upon reflection, though, I noticed that the few results were hardly worth the exhausting and extended effort put into it. But I kept trying, wishfully thinking that they would come round to it. 
This video really exposed me to the truth and mechanics of belief systems in a way that it is not confusing. I understand now my folly in the matter. THANK YOU!

author: @frankbenevolence
text:
I think the predictive value, explanatory value and logical cohesion of any given belief system is a pretty straight forward way to judge beliefs against each other.  That along with quantity of quality evidence.  Just because you can never reach absolute certainty (In a Descartes, "we might be in the matrix" kinda way) doesn't mean all hypotheses are created equal. If the belief doesn't reflect reality or is logically flawed it should be given lower credence.  Given the long line of unscrupulous people willing to prey on the gullible and weaponize the fervor of the self-righteous, I think it's important to be self-critical and really examine why we believe what we believe, with extra scrutiny for the things we take for granted.  
You can trust the process of scientific discovery without having to trust any of the individual scientists involved, because it includes self-correcting mechanisms and produces theories with predictive power unmatched by any other process.  If I roll two 6 sided dice and the whole world believed they would add up to 87, the whole world is just wrong, unless you want invoke grand illusions. Many beliefs are deeply flawed and often harmful and should not be put on equal footing with ideas that have a wide body of evidence supporting them from multiple lines of inquiry.

replies.author: @CelticShae
replies.text:
I think we are starting with that presupposition, though. Even if all religion was washed from the face the Earth, there would still be beliefs that stand outside the scientific point of view. The human mind is a labyrinth of unseen and unacknowledged superstition and morals. I know lots of atheists who are superstitious about certain things.

We can no more dispossess another human of their worldview by proving it wrong than we can argue a horse into drinking water. If what we believe is true, good and right, those who can see it will. Those who can't, we have allowed them more peace in their life, which is a different sort of gift in this world gone mad.

Mind you, I'm not arguing against the scientific method as a foundation for reason, or that it shouldn't be given space in the public discourse. It should be out front where the most people can be impacted by it.

My point is more on a personal level of relationships and in the comments of YouTube videos (or Facebook or whatever), we should worry less about what someone else believes and act more authentically and compassionately toward others.

The likelihood of arguing a theist into atheism in this forum is so close to zero, it might as well be zero. But they might remember your kindness and cordiality in the future when they have found a question their faith can't answer.

author: @mfr58
text: Thank you, that was so well articulated. We live in a consensus reality, some see the around the edges of that into other possible realities and may even become disruptors, leading to a new consensus. Most so called scientifically minded people would do well to reflect on this and eat some humble pie....

author: @musikSkool
text: That was a pretty big theme in Stephen Hawking's book "A Brief History Of Time".  Science progresses when new theories come along to supplant old ones, but he points out that there was nothing wrong with the old theories, as long as they made useful predictions that turned out to be true.

replies.author: @CelticShae
replies.text: We do still use Newtonian Physics, after all. It isn't the best or most accurate, but it was sufficient to get us to the moon.

replies.author: @c3N3q
replies.text:
We all believe something, some believe in something. Nobody knows what was before the "Big Bang",e.g.,  we only Believe it was really small, then it exploded and started working according to all the rules of physics, mathematics and later on biology as we know them today. 
Anyway, with the moon landing, any plausible theory on why we haven't been able to do another one? In all those years? Today, everyone has a computer in their pocket more powerful than the one they had back then. What's stopping us?

replies.author: @frankbenevolence
replies.text: @CelticShae I'm pretty sure Einstein's equations at non-relitvistic speeds are the same as Newton's equations.  It's a good example of a new theory needing to explain the existing body of evidence.
